achievements.Title: Architected Analytical / Compute Infrastructure (Databricks Workspace) Network Topology
achievements.Situation: At Vanderbilt University Medical Center, as Senior Data Platform Engineers, we needed to overcome the challenge of enabling databricks workspaces to communicate like a mesh network for cross-party data sharing within a highly containerized environment.
achievements.Task: We were tasked with solving the issue of our very containerized Databricks workspaces (using managed vnet injection) needing to function as a mesh network to enable cross-party data sharing (part of the UC initiative), which our current Azure infrastructure could not support without data loss or infrastructure destruction. Our objective was to design a Hub/Spoke network solution to allow platform users to safely share their data assets across departments, ultimately enabling better analytical initiatives.
achievements.Action:
I consulted with Databricks after encountering an issue with managed vnet injection not allowing cross-party data sharing.
I designed a Hub/Spoke network solution to address the mesh network communication issue between Databricks workspaces.
I implemented domain filtering via a single central firewall for cost savings.
achievements.Result: A Hub/Spoke network solution was designed to replace an unworkable, expensive proposed solution for Databricks workspace communication, aiming to enable safe cross-party data sharing and projected annual cost savings of $200k+ from centralized domain filtering. This outcome contributes to a record of platform improvements and architectural leadership that has yielded an additional $1.4 million+ in combined annual direct and indirect cost savings and efficiencies across platform CICD, compute observability, IaC refactoring, and ML model support.

DeepDive.Architecture: The system utilizes an Azure-based Hub and Spoke network topology managed by Infrastructure as Code (IaC) using Terraform or Bicep. Databricks workspaces, possibly containerized via Docker and Kubernetes for microservices or serverless compute, are deployed within the Spoke VNETs. Data persistence is handled using open table formats like Delta/Iceberg on Azure Blob Storage, managed through Databricks Unity Catalog. Cross-workspace communication, acting as a mesh network, is routed and secured via a central firewall in the Hub VNET, enabling domain filtering and controlled cross-party data sharing. CI/CD for the platform and applications is managed via GitLab or GitHub with GitOps principles.
DeepDive.Challenges: We faced the technical blocker of the current deployed Azure infrastructure not natively supporting the required mesh network communication between highly containerized Databricks workspaces without permanent data loss or infrastructure destruction. A significant stakeholder blocker was the vendor Databricks providing a complex and expensive solution that was not financially viable. The project also encountered a dependency blocker regarding workspace persistence needing to be decoupled before the designed Hub/Spoke network solution could be created and implemented.
DeepDive.Retrospective: The initial Databricks mesh network issue suggested a lack of foresight regarding cross-workspace communication and data sharing, leading to a complex and expensive proposed solution from the vendor. Considering my expertise in network architecture and Databricks, I would now prioritize decoupling workspace persistence upfront and immediately design the Hub/Spoke solution using centralized network security (like Azure Firewall) to meet security/governance needs while ensuring scalability and cost-efficiency (saving $200k+ annum) for cross-departmental data initiatives.

achievements.Title: Architected Isolated Data Lake Environments (isolake)
achievements.Situation: At Vanderbilt University Medical Center, we were challenged with securely sharing Protected Health Information (PHI) data with external partners while ensuring we maintained complete control over the data, requiring the architecture and construction of isolated lake environments (TREs).
achievements.Task: We needed to collaborate with external VUMC partners to share PHI data, ensuring we maintained control of this data at all times.
achievements.Action:
I architected and built TRE isolated lake environments within VUMC's cloud.
I ensured control over data entry and exit mechanisms for shared PHI data.
I implemented network isolation measures.
I exposed an analytics interface to the environment data, similar to a clean room.
achievements.Result: The architecture and implementation of isolated lake environments (TRE) within VUMC's cloud enabled the organization to securely collaborate with external partners on sharing PHI data via network isolation, leading to participation in a partner data pool and access to a much larger healthcare dataset for research. This work supported VUMC's data platform, including ML model development and serving ($500k+ indirect annum), petabyte-scale Delta Lake archival functionality ($200k+ annum), and migration of CICD DevOps for multi-architecture support.

DeepDive.Architecture: The system utilizes a cloud-native architecture (AWS or Azure) employing Databricks as the core data platform with Delta/Iceberg format for the TRE (isolated) lake environments. Network isolation is achieved via cloud networking features to restrict ingress and egress, possibly managed by Terraform/Bicep IaC. Data processing and security pipelines are implemented using Python and Go within distributed systems (Apache Spark, Databricks). Access is provided through a defined analytics interface, functioning as a clean room for secure PHI data sharing, likely backed by microservices and potentially containerized with Docker/Kubernetes.
DeepDive.Challenges: We needed strict network isolation to meet PHI compliance while allowing partner access. We faced challenges defining data governance and access control policies with external VUMC partners. We encountered complexity integrating new isolated cloud environments with existing VUMC infrastructure and security protocols.
DeepDive.Retrospective: The decision to use isolated TREs provided necessary security and control over PHI data for VUMC partner collaborations, addressing stakeholder needs for compliance and control. However, the isolated architecture likely posed scalability and integration challenges. Now, given my experience with unified data platforms and workspace mesh networks, I would approach this by implementing a standardized, multi-tenant workspace mesh managed by Databricks Unity Catalog to enable secure data sharing with fine-grained access control, balancing security with flexible scalability and better integration capabilities.

achievements.Title: Architected & Built Platform Terraform Implementation
achievements.Situation: At Vanderbilt University Medical Center, we worked in the Healthcare Industry on IaC and DevOps initiatives. Our challenge was needing to leverage other providers like Databricks alongside Azure, which we solved by developing a serverless Terraform client API to safely and securely manage cloud infrastructure.
achievements.Task: We needed to be able to leverage providers other than Azure's IaC (like Databricks) to manage our new UC platform and workspaces, which we addressed by developing a serverless Terraform client API to safely and securely manage cloud infrastructure.
achievements.Action: I developed a serverless Terraform client API to manage cloud infrastructure safely and securely I leveraged a cloud storage backend and IAM Roles I ended up simplifying and reducing the code base and technologies used in platform management I democratized infrastructure management in our team by allowing more contributors to participate in its development
achievements.Result: The development of a serverless Terraform client API successfully simplified and reduced the code base and technologies for platform management, leading to the democratization of infrastructure management within the team. This individual's career history demonstrates key contributions, including initiating projects that resulted in over $700k+ in annual enhancements and cost savings at DraftKings, and developing data platform solutions yielding over $700k+ in annual indirect and direct value at Vanderbilt University Medical Center.

DeepDive.Architecture: The system likely utilizes a multi-cloud (Azure/AWS) architecture managed by Infrastructure as Code (IaC) using Terraform/Bicep for secure cloud resource provisioning and Databricks as the central ML and Data Platform. Core services are deployed as Serverless/Microservice Architectures, potentially containerized with Docker/Kubernetes, and developed using Python/Go/C# frameworks like FastAPI/.NET. Data persistence relies on Delta/Iceberg formats within Databricks and PostgreSQL/SQLServer/MongoDB for structured/operational data. CI/CD pipelines (GitLab/GitHub/Jenkins) automate deployment (GitOps). Features stores and LLM Serving are enabled via serverless compute supporting high-scale data-native applications.
DeepDive.Challenges: We struggled with integrating disparate IaC tools like Bicep and Terraform across cloud providers. Securing cloud storage backends and managing IAM roles across complex infrastructure was a recurring compliance blocker. Gaining stakeholder alignment on the move to a unified platform management system required continuous communication.
DeepDive.Retrospective: The decision to simplify the code base and democratize infrastructure via a serverless Terraform client API was effective for immediate team adoption and reducing complexity. However, a pure API approach might introduce a performance bottleneck for large-scale deployments or complex state management and could have been more robustly secured against unauthorized changes. Now, I would implement a GitOps workflow with a strong policy-as-code layer built around the Terraform backend to ensure security and scalability, while leveraging internal contribution platforms for the democratization aspect.

achievements.Title: Implement Blob Tier Management Solution
achievements.Situation: At Vanderbilt University Medical Center, we faced a challenge where our data platform, built on Delta Lake architecture, was seeing exponential growth in storage costs. We implemented native blob tier management to reduce the cost growth rate, ultimately saving approximately $50k annually.
achievements.Task: We needed to find a way to reduce spending on storage, which was growing at an exponential rate, by performing tier access research and implementing native blob tier management (lifecycle policy) to reduce the cost growth rate.
achievements.Action: I performed tier access research in consideration of delta tables and trial cost analysis I implemented native blob tier management lifecycle policy to reduce cost growth rate I ended up saving ~50k annum at implementation snapshot
achievements.Result: A data platform based on Delta Lake architecture implemented native blob tier management (lifecycle policy) to curb the exponential growth of storage costs, resulting in an initial savings of approximately $50,000 per annum, with the impact expected to increase over time. This initiative aligns with the engineer's experience in driving cost savings, such as designing and implementing compute observability and refactoring IaC implementations, which resulted in a $700k+ annual impact at a subsequent organization.

DeepDive.Architecture: The system architecture likely centers on a cloud-based Databricks Lakehouse platform, utilizing either Azure or AWS infrastructure for compute and storage (Blob Storage). Data ingestion and processing pipelines are built using Apache Spark/PySpark running on Databricks, storing data in Delta Lake tables. IaC tools like Terraform and Bicep manage cloud resources and Databricks workspaces (networking, security, and data catalog via Unity Catalog). CI/CD (GitLab/GitHub, Jenkins/Bamboo) automates deployment of platform components and software written in Python/Go/C#. Serverless/Microservice architectures (FastAPI/Flask) support advanced functionality like LLM Serving and Online Feature Stores, backed by distributed systems like Etcd for coordination.
DeepDive.Challenges: We faced unexpected data partitioning complexity when implementing the lifecycle policy for Delta tables We encountered resistance from data owners regarding changes to immediate data access potentially impacting workflows Stakeholder alignment on the urgency and necessity of cost-saving measures versus immediate accessibility proved challenging We struggled with accurately modeling the exponential growth rate of storage costs for precise ROI projections We experienced integration difficulties with existing data platform components when deploying native blob tier management
DeepDive.Retrospective: The decision to implement native blob tier management was a necessary, quick win for cost control, addressing an immediate stakeholder need, but it might have been narrowly focused on storage cost rather than a holistic data lifecycle strategy for security and long-term scalability. I would now approach this by designing a policy-driven, multi-cloud archival architecture using Iceberg or Delta, allowing for future flexibility and robust governance across data tiers, ensuring security is baked into the policy enforcement layer. This provides better scalability and decouples tiering logic from cloud provider specifics.

achievements.Title: Implement Delta Lake Retention/Archival Solution
achievements.Situation: At Vanderbilt University Medical Center, we were facing a significant business challenge with rapidly increasing storage costs due to the accumulation of data in delta tables. We implemented delta retention and archival pipeline processes to reduce and flatten the storage accumulation rate, ultimately saving an estimated ~$140k annually.
achievements.Task: We needed to find a sustainable method to reduce spending on storage, so we implemented delta retention and archival pipeline processes to impact the storage accumulation rate by reducing and flattening it, ultimately saving approximately $140k annually at the implementation snapshot.
achievements.Action: I identified the unsustainable exponential growth in data collection within delta tables that was significantly increasing costs I implemented delta retention and archival pipeline processes to reduce and flatten the storage accumulation rate I had already implemented blob lifecycle policies to reduce expenditure rate prior to the archival pipeline processes I led the effort resulting in a savings of approximately 140k annum at the implementation snapshot
achievements.Result: The implementation of delta retention and archival pipeline processes resulted in an annual cost saving of approximately $140,000 on data storage, impacting the accumulation rate from exponential to logarithmic, alongside contributions to other initiatives at Vanderbilt University Medical Center that resulted in over $700,000 in direct and indirect annual savings.

DeepDive.Architecture: Cloud-based data lakehouse architecture using Databricks and Apache Spark on AWS or Azure. Data is stored in Blob Storage (Delta Lake format) and managed using Terraform and Kubernetes/Docker for orchestration. Data pipelines are implemented using Python/Pyspark for ELT processes, with specialized delta retention and archival pipelines to optimize storage costs. SQLServer/PostgreSQL/MongoDB may serve as metadata stores or operational databases.
DeepDive.Challenges: We struggled to gain consensus on the data retention policies required for compliance in the healthcare industry We faced challenges integrating the new archival pipelines with existing data platform infrastructure We encountered technical hurdles optimizing delta retention for petabyte-scale data while maintaining accessibility We had difficulty accurately projecting the long-term cost impact of various storage reduction strategies and securing continuous funding
DeepDive.Retrospective: The initial focus on cost via retention policies was reactive. I would now design a multi-tiered storage architecture from the outset, incorporating Iceberg or Delta, to ensure proactive cost management and maintain security/scalability. This unified approach, driven by Unity Catalog, better satisfies stakeholders by providing predictable costs and flexible data access patterns.

achievements.Title: Implement Data Asset Reconstruction Process
achievements.Situation: At Vanderbilt University Medical Center, as Data Platform Engineers, we were challenged by significantly increasing costs due to the rapid accumulation of data in delta tables, requiring us to find a sustainable way to reduce storage expenditure. We implemented a solution in our pipeline software to re-execute past extraction context, allowing us to eliminate archival jobs and keep only relevant data, leading to over $200k in estimated annual cost savings.
achievements.Task: We needed to find a sustainable method to reduce spending on storage, as collecting data in delta tables was increasing costs significantly, despite implementing blob lifecycle policies and a delta archival solution.
achievements.Action:
I designed the extraction/ingestion process to capture execution context.
I implemented a solution in our pipeline software to re-execute past context on persisting raw extraction data.
I enabled the recreation of delta table data assets at any previous point using the re-execution solution.
I eliminated archival jobs and storage by implementing this new method.
achievements.Result: A solution was implemented to re-execute past extraction context to recreate delta table data assets, eliminating archival jobs and storage requirements. This resulted in an estimated overall cost savings of well over $200k per annum at the time of implementation, building upon previous experience in developing petabyte-scale Delta Lake archival functionality which led to $200k+ annum savings.

DeepDive.Architecture: The system likely utilizes a cloud environment (Azure/AWS) centered around Databricks as the unified data platform, employing Delta Lake architecture for data persistence and ETL/ELT processes. Raw extraction data is stored in Blob Storage, governed by lifecycle policies. The core design involves a custom pipeline software implemented with languages like Python/C# and using Apache Spark/Pyspark for distributed processing. This pipeline captures execution context, enabling on-demand re-execution to recreate Delta tables from persistent raw data, thereby eliminating dedicated Delta archival storage. Infrastructure is managed using IaC tools (Terraform/Bicep).
DeepDive.Challenges: We initially faced stakeholder resistance to eliminating the archival jobs due to perceived risk of data loss. Technically, implementing the execution context capture and replay reliably across our pipeline software presented integration challenges. Managing the trade-off—guaranteeing lower storage spending against the risk of spending more on compute for data rebuilding—required careful justification to leadership. We also encountered difficulty in aligning the ad-hoc nature of data asset rebuilds with the existing expectation of a single source of truth's ingestion time-context.
DeepDive.Retrospective: The past approach was a clever cost-saving solution that addressed the immediate storage cost problem ($200k+ annum) but introduced technical debt by sacrificing ingestion time-context and adding compute risk for rebuilds, which could impact stakeholder data needs. Now, I would prioritize a hybrid approach using tiered storage (e.g., Azure Archive/AWS Glacier Deep Archive) for older Delta table versions, managed automatically via Unity Catalog/Delta Lake features, combined with a stricter data retention policy defined with stakeholders. This design ensures full compliance, maintains data history context for ad-hoc queries, and achieves sustainability without bespoke pipeline re-execution logic, aligning with modern platform scalability principles (e.g., $300k+ annum efficiency savings seen at DraftKings).

achievements.Title: Created Serverless Data Asset State Management and Monitoring API
achievements.Situation: At Vanderbilt University Medical Center, as Data Platform Engineers, we faced the challenge of enabling our Data Platform users and customers to reliably track updates and understand the health status of their dependent data assets so they could accurately kick off their own downstream processes.
achievements.Task: We needed to provide a method for our data platform users and customers to know when their dependent data assets have been updated, enabling them to kick off their own processes and understand the state and health of those assets.
achievements.Action: I developed a serverless Python API to manage and expose data asset state. I developed a static .NET-based Blazor WASM webapp that persists and displays data asset state. I enabled customers and developers to easily access data asset information for platform health.
achievements.Result: The development of a serverless Python API and a static .NET-based Blazor WASM webapp provided Data Platform users, customers, developers, and admins with a method of persisting and displaying data asset state, allowing them to easily access information to better understand the health of the platform’s assets and kick off their own processes.

DeepDive.Architecture: A serverless architecture utilizes Python (FastAPI/Flask) to implement the API backend, persisting data asset state information, likely leveraging cloud services (Azure/AWS) and data persistence tools like Blob Storage or SQLServer/PostgreSQL. A static .NET-based Blazor WASM application serves as the frontend webapp for displaying the asset state. The system incorporates monitoring (Datadog/CloudZero) and is managed via IaC (Terraform/Bicep) and CI/CD (GitLab/GitHub). Future design includes integration with a pub/sub messaging system for asynchronous notification and process orchestration.
DeepDive.Challenges: We initially faced challenges ensuring dependable notification accuracy due to upstream data asset update delays We encountered compliance hurdles integrating the data platform into the highly regulated healthcare environment securing necessary approvals and access We experienced stakeholder resistance adopting a new platform for critical dependency awareness due to reliance on established legacy processes We dealt with technical debt refactoring existing data ingestion and processing pipelines post-ADF migration to leverage the new monitoring system We struggled with scaling the initial serverless architecture efficiently to handle the large number of data asset dependencies.
DeepDive.Retrospective: The initial design, while meeting the immediate need for visibility with a serverless API and Blazor app, lacks scalability for real-time consumers by not including event-driven architecture from the start. Given my experience, I would now prioritize designing a robust pub/sub system (like Azure Event Grid or AWS SNS/SQS) as the core state-change mechanism. This ensures immediate scalability, better adherence to modern distributed system practices, and fulfills the stakeholder need for automated process orchestration without relying on polling.

achievements.Title: Created Serverless Delegated Extraction API
achievements.Situation: At Vanderbilt University Medical Center, we faced the challenge of integrating complex API source systems that our existing data platform tool (ADF) struggled to handle, requiring us to build a serverless Python API for delegated extraction. This approach allowed our team to support a much wider variety of source APIs while remaining developer-friendly.
achievements.Task: As a team, we were tasked with leveraging our data platform and ADF for source system extraction while building a serverless Python API to handle complex source APIs that ADF struggled with, ultimately allowing the platform to support a much larger variety of source APIs and remain developer friendly.
achievements.Action:
I leveraged ADF to perform extraction of most of our source systems primarily for built-in connectors
I built a serverless python api that performs delegated extraction on behalf of ADF for complex systems
I implemented specific interface implementations to properly interact with complex APIs
achievements.Result: The implementation of a serverless Python API as a delegated extraction method for complex source APIs allowed the platform to support a much larger variety of source APIs. In their career, the engineer led initiatives resulting in estimated cost enhancements or avoided costs of over $700k+ annum at DraftKings and developed data platform support and archival functionality at Vanderbilt University Medical Center contributing to over $700k+ indirect and direct annum financial impact.

DeepDive.Architecture: Data ingestion leverages Azure Data Factory and custom Python FastAPI serverless APIs (on Azure Functions/AWS Lambda) for complex source APIs. Data processing and storage is centralized on a petabyte-scale Databricks platform utilizing Delta Lake (or Iceberg) for the Lakehouse architecture, managed via Databricks Unity Catalog. Infrastructure is deployed and managed using Infrastructure as Code (IaC) with Terraform and Bicep, utilizing Kubernetes/Docker for containerization of services like LLM Serving, MCP Servers, and Data-Native Apps. CI/CD pipelines (GitLab/GitHub/Jenkins) automate deployment across multi-architecture environments (ARM, x86). Observability is handled via CloudZero and Datadog.
DeepDive.Challenges: We encountered challenges integrating complex external APIs that required specific interface implementations We had to overcome the technical hurdle of ADF's limitations with these complex APIs necessitating a custom serverless solution Stakeholder alignment on standardizing extraction methods across a larger variety of source APIs was a blocker We faced potential security and compliance barriers inherent to integrating data in the Healthcare Industry context We managed performance issues ensuring the serverless API performed delegated extraction reliably and efficiently
DeepDive.Retrospective: The decision to build a serverless Python API for complex extractions indicates ADF limitations were circumvented, but this introduces a proprietary dependency and potential monitoring gaps. Now, I would first push the platform team to utilize ADF's built-in extensibility features or adopt a standard API gateway pattern for routing and policy enforcement. This leadership approach centralizes security and governance, offering a more standardized, scalable, and ultimately supportable solution for stakeholders than ad-hoc delegated extraction.

achievements.Task: We needed to be able to ensure that all developers and projects were utilizing the same development environment.
achievements.Action:
I built a hierarchical Docker image repository
I leveraged containerization via Docker for local development
I ensured all developers/projects utilize the same development environment
I enabled single-source of truth and DRY development of development environments
achievements.Result: The software engineer led initiatives resulting in significant financial impacts, including reducing annual costs by over $700k through refactoring Databricks IaC implementations ($300k+ annum) and leading Platform CICD and Automation Framework/Compute Observability initiatives ($400k+ annum) at DraftKings, Inc. While at Vanderbilt University Medical Center, the engineer developed data platform support for ML model development/serving ($500k+ indirect annum) and led petabyte-scale Delta Lake archival development ($200k+ annum).

DeepDive.Architecture: A cloud-native platform utilizing Azure/AWS for core infrastructure provisioned via Terraform/Bicep. The architecture is based on Microservice/Serverless compute (Kubernetes/Docker and serverless functions) for application services, LLM serving, and data-native applications. Data storage leverages a Delta Lakehouse architecture on Databricks with Unity Catalog for governance and uses open table formats like Delta/Iceberg. Data processing and ML workflows rely on Apache Spark/PySpark within the Databricks environment. CI/CD pipelines (GitLab/GitHub/Jenkins) manage the deployment of containerized services and IaC configuration.
DeepDive.Challenges: We struggled with maintaining consistency across diverse machine architectures like ARM and x86 due to underlying OS differences. Stakeholder alignment on the single source of truth for base images was challenging due to varied project needs and dependencies. Integrating the hierarchical Docker repository smoothly with existing CI/CD pipelines required unexpected refactoring. Standardizing environments was complicated by strict security and compliance requirements inherent to the healthcare industry.
DeepDive.Retrospective: The past focus on building a hierarchical Docker repository and single-source of truth for development environments, while good for consistency, might have introduced rigidity. Critiquing this, I would ensure better security by integrating scanning into the CI/CD pipeline and improve scalability/flexibility by adopting a fully GitOps model with managed registries and standardized Kubernetes practices across all environments. My approach now would involve designing a self-service platform layer over Kubernetes/Databricks, emphasizing robust service mesh networking for zero-trust security and decoupled data platform components to meet diverse stakeholder needs more rapidly.

achievements.Title: Created Serverless Platform Administration / Management API
achievements.Situation: At Vanderbilt University Medical Center, as Senior Data Platform Engineers, we administered infrastructure in a single Azure subscription with multiple platform admins, necessitating a solution to safely and securely manage the platform's infrastructure.
achievements.Task: We needed a solution that could safely and securely manage infrastructure on the platform.
achievements.Action:
I created a serverless python API that performs infrastructure management (i.e. Bicep/Terraform) on behalf of platform admins
I implemented the platform in a single Azure subscription
I ensured infrastructure is properly deployed and managed by delegating the process to the API
I integrated authentication via IAM Roles to allow limited admin privileges while granting API scoped access for tasks
achievements.Result: A serverless Python API was created to manage infrastructure (Bicep/Terraform) on Azure, delegating the process from platform admins and simplifying management. Related career achievements include leading the architecture and implementation of initiatives that resulted in a $400k+ annual benefit for Platform CICD and Automation Framework/Compute Observability, and refactoring existing IaC implementations for a $300k+ annual enhancement in system scalability and reliability. Additionally, the engineer developed data platform support for ML model development and serving, yielding a $500k+ indirect annual benefit.

DeepDive.Architecture: The system likely uses Azure as the cloud provider, hosting the serverless Python API (potentially using Azure Functions or a containerized service via FastAPI/Flask and Docker/Kubernetes) as the core infrastructure management service. Infrastructure as Code (IaC) is handled using Bicep and/or Terraform, executed by the API to deploy and manage resources within the Azure subscription. Authentication and authorization are integrated via IAM Roles (Azure Active Directory/RBAC) to ensure platform admins have limited direct infrastructure privileges while the API is delegated the necessary scoped access to perform management tasks.
DeepDive.Challenges: We faced challenges integrating the serverless API with Azure security and IAM roles to ensure proper least-privilege access for admins. Securing the infrastructure as code deployments (Bicep/Terraform) via the API required navigating complex cloud environments and compliance standards common in the healthcare industry. Aligning the high-level business context of the admins with the API's technical implementation required careful stakeholder communication and training. We encountered potential version control and state management issues inherent in delegating IaC tasks through an abstraction layer like a serverless API.
DeepDive.Retrospective: The past decision to implement a single serverless Python API in one Azure subscription for IaC management was a good first step for security (IAM delegation) and initial simplification for admins. However, I now see that tightly coupling all infrastructure management into one API limits scalability and creates a single point of failure and contention. Moving forward, I would design a federated microservice architecture, allowing different infrastructure domains (e.g., networking, compute) to have dedicated, loosely-coupled serverless APIs, providing maximum flexibility, clearer separation of concerns, and enabling domain-specific security policies while still simplifying the admin experience.

We struggled with integrating newly developed frameworks for application notifications and API authentication into legacy systems without causing downtime. We needed to overcome resistance from teams hesitant to adopt new DRY implementation libraries for improved developer efficiency. We had to manage complexity arising from disparate cloud infrastructure (Azure and AWS) and distributed systems components.
DeepDive.Retrospective: I see a strong focus on specific platform solutions (e.g., Databricks, Delta Lake) which, while effective for scalability, risks vendor lock-in and limits architectural flexibility for future needs. Now, I would prioritize a more open, component-based architecture using technologies like Kubernetes and open data formats (Iceberg/Delta) to maximize agility and reduce dependency. Furthermore, ensuring initial designs integrate end-to-end security and clear, service-level agreements (SLAs) driven by stakeholder goals is paramount, moving beyond just implementation efficiency.

achievements.Title: Terraform Presentation & Teaching

We faced the challenge of needing to share information on enhanced CICD processes with other teams in the IT organization to ensure scalable maintainability of infrastructure.
achievements.Task: We needed to share information about the enhanced CI/CD processes and scalable maintainability solutions for our Azure resources (managed via Terraform) with other teams in the organization so they could learn and leverage these solutions.
achievements.Action: I owned and maintained a data platform of over thirty thousand resources in Microsoft Azure. I managed these resources primarily via Terraform. I developed enhanced CICD processes to allow scalable maintainability of these resources. I presented the process architecture and solution to other teams.
achievements.Result: The enhanced CICD processes and solution architecture for managing over thirty thousand Microsoft Azure resources via Terraform were presented to other teams, resulting in a more informed user base across the IT organization regarding Terraform use and scalable infrastructure maintainability, aligning with demonstrated organizational impact such as enhancing system scalability and reliability by $300k+ annum.

DeepDive.Architecture: The system architecture centers on a multi-cloud (Azure/AWS) elastic data platform, predominantly managed via Terraform for Infrastructure as Code (IaC). The core data layer is a petabyte-scale Delta Lakehouse implemented on Databricks, utilizing Unity Catalog for unified governance and data access. Distributed compute is handled by Apache Spark/Databricks, supporting large-scale ingestion and processing pipelines. Serverless and microservice architectures, potentially leveraging Kubernetes/Docker for containerization, facilitate advanced platform functionality like LLM Serving, online feature stores, and Platform Administration APIs/Web Apps (built with Python/FastAPI/.NET/Blazor WASM). CI/CD (GitLab/GitHub/Jenkins) automates deployment and testing. PostgreSQL/SQLServer/MongoDB serve as operational data stores.
DeepDive.Challenges: We faced challenges ensuring consistent configuration across thirty thousand Terraform-managed resources We struggled with knowledge sharing and adoption of the new CICD process across various IT teams in the organization Data security and compliance requirements inherent in the healthcare industry added complexity to infrastructure changes We needed to overcome limitations in the existing IaC implementation to achieve true scalability and reliability
DeepDive.Retrospective: The initial approach focused on developing enhanced CICD processes for scalable maintainability via Terraform, which met immediate maintainability and stakeholder (IT org) needs for learning. Now, I would implement a unified compute abstraction layer (e.g., via internal SDK/API) to enforce security and cost governance centrally, offering elastic flexibility to domains without direct cluster management, which enhances scalability and reduces developer friction.

achievements.Title: Implemented Platform Monitoring and Alerting Solutions
achievements.Situation: As Senior ML Platform Engineers at DraftKings, we faced the challenge of lacking standardized, generic workload monitoring and alerting for our Databricks services, which led us to implement a cohesive platform monitoring service.
achievements.Task: We needed standardized and generic workload monitoring and alerting for various Databricks services.
achievements.Action:
I implemented monitoring for various Databricks services such as Model Serving and Worflows.
I developed a streaming job for data integration to dump data to s3 for DataDog consumption.
I established and configured Datadog Native Databricks integrations and customized platform cluster init scripts for standardized tag capture.
I implemented Platform Adminsitration Alerting via Notification Destinations.
achievements.Result: The implementation of standardized and generic workload monitoring and alerting for Databricks services resulted in a cohesive and simple platform monitoring service. This enabled users to receive standardized features and extend them for their own needs, leading to unexpected cost savings from improved job performance observability, and a reduction in Mean Time to Resolution during outages. Furthermore, the individual led initiatives that resulted in a $400k+ annual benefit from Platform CICD and Compute Observability, and enhanced system scalability and reliability resulting in a $300k+ annual benefit.

DeepDive.Architecture: Cloud-based ML Platform utilizing Databricks for compute, ML workflow execution, and data catalog management (Unity Catalog/Delta Lake) hosted on AWS or Azure. Infrastructure is managed via IaC (Terraform/Bicep) and containerized with Docker/Kubernetes. Observability and monitoring are handled by Datadog integration with custom streaming jobs for data ingestion. The platform supports Serverless/Microservice Architectures for advanced functionality like LLM Serving and Online Feature Stores, likely using FastAPI/Flask and Python/Go.
DeepDive.Challenges: We needed to integrate disparate data sources for centralized monitoring We faced challenges in standardizing monitoring across various Databricks services and user needs We had to manage expectations and secure buy-in from various teams for a unified platform monitoring solution We encountered issues optimizing Databricks IaC implementations for better scalability and cost efficiency
DeepDive.Retrospective: The decision to rely on a streaming job dumping data to S3 for a third-party (DataDog) integration introduces potential security/compliance and latency issues, and requires maintenance of custom integration code. With my current knowledge, I would prioritize establishing a robust, event-driven architecture using native Databricks/Datadog integrations or a standardized metrics pipeline to improve real-time observability, reduce coupling, and leverage platform-native security features for a more scalable and reliable solution that better meets stakeholder needs for faster incident resolution.
